findings.description: Docstring claims strict YYYY-MM-DD parsing, but datetime.strptime with %Y-%m-%d accepts non-zero-padded fields (e.g. '2024-1-5') and variable-width years, violating the stated strict-format contract.
day_range materializes one date object per day with no upper bound, so a wide untrusted range (e.g. years 1-9999) allocates millions of objects, an unbounded memory/DoS risk.

business_days_between builds the full day_range list purely to count weekdays, using O(n) memory and allocation where an O(1) arithmetic computation would suffice, amplifying day_range's unbounded-allocation issue.

clamp_to_range compares value/lower/upper with </min/max but, unlike humanize_delta, never validates tzinfo, so mixing naive and aware datetimes raises an undocumented TypeError instead of the documented ValueError.
humanize_delta only checks tzinfo is not None, but a tzinfo whose utcoffset() returns None still passes the guard and can produce incorrect or failing subtraction results.
humanize_delta's coarsest bucket is weeks, so deltas of months or years render as large week counts (e.g. '520 w ago'), a degraded human-readable contract for old timestamps.
start_of_day accepts a datetime (a date subclass) and silently discards its time and tzinfo components without flagging the type mismatch against the date-typed contract.
